title: Agathosma parviflora
taxonomy: Agathosma parviflora is classified as a species of plant within the family Rutaceae, commonly known as the citrus or rue family. This classification is based on the plant's botanical characteristics and evolutionary relationships with other species. The Rutaceae family is a diverse group of flowering plants that includes various genera, such as Citrus, Fortunella, and Zanthoxylum. Agathosma parviflora, in particular, is a species of shrub native to the Western Cape Province in South Africa. Within the Rutaceae family, Agathosma parviflora belongs to the genus Agathosma, which comprises several species of plants.
uses: Agathosma parviflora, also known as the small-flowered buchuberg, is used in traditional medicine, particularly in South Africa. It is valued for its potential to treat various health issues, including respiratory problems and skin conditions. The plant's leaves and stems are used to make a tea that is said to have anti-inflammatory properties. intro: Agathosma parviflora, also known as Cape plume or buchu, is a low-growing, shrubby plant native to the Western Cape province of South Africa. It is valued for its medicinal properties, which include its ability to relieve digestive issues, reduce inflammation, and exhibit antimicrobial activity. The bioactive constituents of Agathosma parviflora include essential oils, particularly borneol and borneol acetate, as well as phenolic compounds such as rosmarinic acid and caffeic acid. Traditionally, buchu is used in various herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are often used to treat conditions such as indigestion, fever, and urinary tract infections.
